title: Work, Energy and Power Test - 1 (Topic Test)

questions.questionText: A mass of 1 kg is kept on a inclined plane with $30^{\circ}$ inclination with respect to horizontal plane and it is at rest initially. Then the whole assembly is moved up with constant velocity of $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The work done by the frictional force in time 2 s is $\_\_\_\_$ J. (Take $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
When the block is at rest on the incline, the forces acting on it are :

$\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{g}}=\mathrm{mg}$ is weight acting vertically downward.

$\mathrm{N}=$ Normal force acting perpendicular to the incline.

$\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}}=$ Static friction acting up the incline, preventing the block from sliding down.

Since the block is at rest relative to the incline, the frictional force must balance the component of gravity acting down the slope :

$$ \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{mg} \sin \theta $$

The mass of block is $\mathrm{m}=1 \mathrm{~kg}$, acceleration due to gravity is $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and $\theta=30^{\circ}$

So, value of static friction is

$$ \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}}=1 \times 10 \times \sin 30^{\circ}=10 \times 0.5=5 \mathrm{~N} $$

The friction force is 5 N directed up along the incline.

As the entire assembly moves vertically upward with a constant velocity $\mathrm{v}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, so in time $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}$, the vertical displacement $S$ is:

$$ S=v \times t=4 \times 2=8 \mathrm{~m} $$

Work done is defined as $W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}=F d \cos \phi$, where $\phi$ is the angle between the force and the displacement.

The angle between the friction force and the vertical is $90^{\circ}-30^{\circ}=60^{\circ}=\phi$.

So, work done by frictional force is,

$$ \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}} \times \mathrm{S} \times \cos 60^{\circ} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{W}=5 \times 8 \times 0.5 \mathrm{~J}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{W}=5 \times 4=20 \mathrm{~J} $$

Therefore, the work done by the frictional force is 20 J .

Hence, the correct option is (A).

questions.questionText: A particle is moving in a circular path of radius $$a$$ under the action of an attractive potential $$U =  - {k \over {2{r^2}}}$$ Its total energy is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
We know, Total energy = Kinetic energy + Potential energy

Potential energy given as $$U = - {k \over {2{r^2}}}$$

We need to find Kinetic Energy.

As Force acting on the particle (F) = $$ - {{dU} \over {dr}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow F = - {d \over {dr}}\left( {{{ - k} \over {2{r^2}}}} \right)$$

$$= {k \over 2} \times \left( { - 2} \right) \times {r^{ - 3}}$$

$$ = - {k \over {{r^3}}}$$

Because of this force particle is having circular motion so it will provide possible centripetal force.

$$\left| F \right| = {{m{v^2}} \over r}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{m{v^2}} \over r} = {k \over {{r^3}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$m{v^2} = {k \over {{r^2}}}$$

We know kinetic energy of particle, K = $${1 \over 2}m{v^2}$$ = $${k \over {2{r^2}}}$$

As Total energy = Kinetic energy + Potential energy

So Total energy = $${k \over {2{r^2}}}$$ $$ - {k \over {2{r^2}}}$$ = 0

questions.questionText: A sand dropper drops sand of mass m(t) on a conveyer belt at a rate proportional to the square root of speed (v) of the belt, i.e., $\frac{dm}{dt} \propto \sqrt{v}$. If P is the power delivered to run the belt at constant speed then which of the following relationship is true?
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Given, $${{dm} \over {dt}} \propto \sqrt v $$

So, $${{dm} \over {dt}} = k\sqrt v $$ .... (i)

where, k = constant

We know, Power = $$P = FV$$

$$ \Rightarrow P = {{dp} \over {dt}}v$$ (As $$F = {{dp} \over {dt}}$$ where, p = linear momentum)

$$ \Rightarrow P = {d \over {dt}}(mv)v$$

$$ \Rightarrow P = v{{dm} \over {dt}}\,.\,v$$ (As speed is constant i.e. v = constant)

$$ \Rightarrow P = {v^2}(k\sqrt v )$$ [From (i)]

$$ \Rightarrow P = k{v^{5/2}}$$

by squaring both sides,

$$ \Rightarrow {P^2} = {k^2}{v^5}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {P^2} \propto {v^5}$$ (As $\mathrm{k^2}$ = constant)

Hence, option 4 is correct.

questions.questionText: A uniform chain of length 3 meter and mass 3 kg overhangs a smooth table with 2 meter lying on the table. If k is the kinetic energy of the chain in joule as it completely slips off the table, then the value of k is ................. . (Take g = 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>)
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
From energy conservation

Ki + Ui = kf + Uf

$$0 + \left( { - 1 \times 10 \times {1 \over 2}} \right) = {k_f} + \left( { - 3 \times 10 \times {3 \over 2}} \right)$$

$$-$$5 = kf $$-$$ 45

kf = 40 J

questions.questionText: An object is projected with kinetic energy $K$ from a point $A$ at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The ratio of the difference in kinetic energies at points $B$ and $C$ to that at point $A$ (see figure), in the absence of air friction is :
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the object be m and the initial speed be u .

The angle of projection is $\theta=60^{\circ}$.

At point A

The kinetic energy at point $A$ is given as $K$.

$$ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^2=\mathrm{K} $$

At Point B (Maximum Height)

At the highest point of a projectile's path, the vertical component of velocity becomes zero $\left(v_y=0\right)$. The object only possesses the horizontal component of velocity ( $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}$ ).

The horizontal velocity remains constant throughout the motion (assuming no air resistance):

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{u} \cos \theta $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{u} \cos 60^{\circ}=\mathrm{u}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{u}}{2} $$

So, the kinetic energy at B is,

$$ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}\right)^2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{u}}{2}\right)^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{4}\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^2\right)=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{4}$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{4} $$

At Point C

Point C is at the same horizontal level as point A . By the law of conservation of energy, the speed of the projectile when it hits the ground at the same level is equal to the speed of projection.

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{u} $$

Therefore, the kinetic energy at C is equal to the kinetic energy at A :

$$ \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{K} $$

The difference in kinetic energies at B and C :

$$ \Delta K_{B C}=\left|K_C-K_B\right|=\left|K-\frac{K}{4}\right|=\frac{3 K}{4} $$

So, the ratio of the difference in kinetic energies at points B and C to that at point A is

Ratio $=\frac{\Delta K_{B C}}{K_A}=\frac{(3 / 4) K}{K}=\frac{3}{4}$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

questions.questionText: A block of mass m is kept on a platform which starts from rest with constant acceleration g/2 upward, as shown in figure. Work done by normal reaction on block in time is -
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
N $$-$$ mg = $${{mg} \over 2}$$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ N = $${{3mg} \over 2}$$

The distance travelled by the system in time t is

S = ut + $${1 \over 2}a{t^2} = 0 + {1 \over 2}\left( {{g \over 2}} \right){t^2} = {1 \over 2}{g \over 2}{t^2}$$

Now, work done

W = N.S = $$\left( {{3 \over 2}mg} \right)\left( {{1 \over 2}{g \over 2}{t^2}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$  W = $${{3m{g^2}{t^2}} \over 8}$$

questions.questionText: A force $$(3 x^2+2 x-5) \mathrm{N}$$ displaces a body from $$x=2 \mathrm{~m}$$ to $$x=4 \mathrm{~m}$$. Work done by this force is ________ J.
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
To find the work done by the force when the body is displaced from $$x = 2 \, \mathrm{m}$$ to $$x = 4 \, \mathrm{m}$$, we use the formula for work done by a variable force in one dimension, which is the integral of the force with respect to displacement:

$$ W = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F \, dx $$

Given the force $$F(x) = (3x^2 + 2x - 5) \, \mathrm{N}$$ and the limits of integration from $$x = 2 \, \mathrm{m}$$ to $$x = 4 \, \mathrm{m}$$, we can substitute these values into the equation:

$$ W = \int_{2}^{4} (3x^2 + 2x - 5) \, dx $$

Calculating the integral, we get:

$$ W = \left[\frac{3x^3}{3} + \frac{2x^2}{2} - 5x\right]_2^4 $$

This simplifies to:

$$ W = \left[x^3 + x^2 - 5x\right]_2^4 $$

Substituting the upper limit ($$x = 4$$) and then the lower limit ($$x = 2$$) into the antiderivative, and subtracting the latter from the former, we get:

$$ W = \left[(4)^3 + (4)^2 - 5(4)\right] - \left[(2)^3 + (2)^2 - 5(2)\right] $$

$$ W = (64 + 16 - 20) - (8 + 4 - 10) $$

$$ W = 60 - 2 $$

$$ W = 58 \, \mathrm{J} $$

Therefore, the work done by the force as the body displaces from $$x = 2 \, \mathrm{m}$$ to $$x = 4 \, \mathrm{m}$$ is $$58 \, \mathrm{J}$$.

questions.questionText:
A porter lifts a heavy suitcase of mass 80 kg and at the destination lowers it down by a distance of 80 cm with a constant velocity. Calculate the work done by the porter in lowering the suitcase.

(take g = 9.8 ms$$-$$2)
questions.subject: Physics

questions.questionText:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason $\mathbf{R}$

Assertion A: In a central force field, the work done is independent of the path chosen.

Reason R: Every force encountered in mechanics does not have an associated potential energy.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options:
$\mathbf{A}$ is false but $\mathbf{R}$ is true
Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are true but $\mathbf{R}$ is NOT the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$
$\mathbf{A}$ is true but $\mathbf{R}$ is false
Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are true and $\mathbf{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution: In a central force field, the work done is independent of the path chosen, this is true because central forces are conservative forces. Every force encountered in mechanics does not have an associated potential energy this is also true because not all forces are conservative. Hence, option 2 is correct.

questions.questionText: A bead $P$ sliding on a frictionless semi-circular string $(A C B)$ and it is at point $S$ at $t =0$ and at this instant the horizontal component of its velocity is $v$. Another bead $Q$ of the same mass as $P$ is ejected from point $A$ at $t=0$ along the horizontal string $A B$, with the speed $v$, friction between the beads and the respective strings may be neglected in both cases. Let $t_P$ and $t_Q$ be the respective times taken by beads $P$ and $Q$ to reach the point $B$, then the relation between $t_P$ and $t_Q$ is
questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
For bead Q

Bead $Q$ moves along the horizontal straight string $A B$. The length $A B$ is $2 R$ where $R$ is the radius of semicircular path.

Since the string is frictionless and horizontal, there is no component of gravity acting along the direction of motion.

Bead Q maintains a constant horizontal velocity v throughout the motion.

$$ t_Q=\frac{A B}{v}=2\left[\frac{R}{v}\right] $$

For bead P

Bead P moves along the semi-circular path ACB under the influence of gravity. And along horizontal the net force is zero, i.e., the acceleration along vertical will be non-zero and the acceleration along horizontal will be zero.

At point S , the horizontal component of its velocity is given as v .

As bead P moves from S toward the bottom-most point C , the vertical component of velocity changes but the horizontal component remains same.

When bead P reaches point B then the horizontal displacement is $\mathrm{S}^{\prime} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{OB}$

$$ \mathrm{S}^{\prime} \mathrm{O}=\mathrm{R} \cos 45^{\circ} $$

Then the time taken to reach point $B$ is $t_P=\frac{S^{\prime} B}{v}=\frac{R \cos 45^{\circ}+R}{v}=\frac{R}{v}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+1\right]$

So, the ratio of time taken by $Q$ and $P$ to reach $B$ is,

$\frac{t_Q}{t_P}=\frac{2[R / v]}{\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+1\right][R / v]}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}+1}=[8-2 \sqrt{2}]>1$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\frac{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{Q}}}{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{P}}}>1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{Q}}>\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{P}}$

questions.questionText: A body of mass $$5 \mathrm{~kg}$$ is moving with a momentum of $$10 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$. Now a force of $$2 \mathrm{~N}$$ acts on the body in the direction of its motion for $$5 \mathrm{~s}$$. The increase in the Kinetic energy of the body is ___________ $$\mathrm{J}$$.
questions.subject: Physics

questions.questionText: A spherical body of mass 2 kg starting from rest acquires a kinetic energy of 10000 J at the end of $$\mathrm{5^{th}}$$ second. The force acted on the body is ________ N.
questions.subject: Physics

questions.questionText: A small particle moves to position $$5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$$ from its initial position $$2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}$$ under the action of force $$5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k} \mathrm{~N}$$. The value of work done will be __________ J.
questions.subject: Physics

questions.questionText:
A ball of mass 100 g is dropped from a height h = 10 cm on a platform fixed at the top of a vertical spring (as shown in figure). The ball stays on the platform and the platform is depressed by a distance $${h \over 2}$$. The spring constant is _____________ Nm$$-$$1.

(Use g = 10 ms$$-$$2)
questions.subject: Physics

questions.questionText: A car accelerates from rest to $$u \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$. The energy spent in this process is E J. The energy required to accelerate the car from $$u \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$ to $$2 \mathrm{u} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$$ is $$\mathrm{nE~J}$$. The value of $$\mathrm{n}$$ is ____________.
questions.subject: Physics
